name: Wright-Patterson Hangars
description: The Wright-Patterson Hangars theory centers on claims that Wright-Patterson Air Force Base in Ohio houses extraterrestrial materials, including crashed UFOs and alien bodies, recovered by the U.S. government. Proponents believe the base serves as a central repository for reverse-engineering alien technology. This theory originated in the mid-20th century, fueled by events like the Roswell incident and Project Blue Book, which was headquartered at Wright-Patterson. Alleged whistleblowers and leaked documents are cited as evidence. Mainstream perspectives dismiss these claims as lacking verifiable evidence, attributing them to misinterpretations of classified military projects, psychological phenomena, and hoaxes. Skeptics point to the absence of physical proof and the reliance on anecdotal accounts as reasons for skepticism. Official government investigations have consistently denied the presence of extraterrestrial materials at the base.
subtitle: Alleged repository of alien technology.

claims.evidence: Alleged whistleblower testimonies, leaked documents (often unverifiable), and anecdotal accounts.
claims.criticism: No verifiable physical evidence exists. Whistleblower accounts are often contradictory and lack corroboration. Leaked documents are frequently debunked as forgeries or misinterpretations of unrelated material.

claims.claim: The base is used for reverse-engineering alien technology.
claims.evidence: Claims of secret underground facilities and specialized research teams dedicated to understanding alien technology.
claims.criticism: The existence of such facilities is unproven. The complexity of advanced technology makes reverse-engineering without prior knowledge highly improbable. Alternative explanations exist for technological advancements attributed to reverse-engineering.

claims.claim: Alien bodies are stored at Wright-Patterson.
claims.evidence: Claims of eyewitness accounts of alien bodies being transported to the base after UFO crashes.
claims.criticism: No photographic or video evidence supports these claims. Eyewitness accounts are unreliable and subject to misinterpretation. Autopsies or scientific studies of alleged alien remains have never been made public or verified by independent sources.

keyFigures.name: Jesse Marcel
keyFigures.role: Intelligence officer involved in the Roswell incident; his changing accounts fueled speculation.

keyFigures.name: Stanton Friedman
keyFigures.role: Ufologist who promoted the Roswell crash and government cover-up theories.

keyFigures.name: Philip J. Klass
keyFigures.role: Journalist and UFO skeptic who debunked many UFO claims, including those related to Wright-Patterson.

mythologyConnections: Connections to stories of encounters with otherworldly beings

timeline.event: Roswell incident; speculation about crashed UFOs begins.
Project Blue Book, the Air Force's UFO investigation, headquartered at Wright-Patterson AFB.
Alleged whistleblower testimonies and leaked documents emerge, fueling the hangar theories.

sources.author: Stanton Friedman
sources.work: Crash at Corona: The U.S. Military Retrieval and Cover-Up of a UFO
sources.title: Stanton Friedman

sources.title: Curtis Peebles
